data.title: Corvus jumps as Goldman Sachs initiates at Buy on lead asset
data.description:
Shares of Corvus Pharmaceuticals (CRVS) spiked on Friday after Goldman Sachs launched its coverage with a Buy recommendation and a $40 target, calling the biotech’s lead candidate, soquelitinib, a pipeline-in-a-product.

Analyst Paul Choi's bullish view comes at a time when shares of the South San Francisco, California-based company have climbed more than 400% over the past 12 months.

However, Choi argued that Corvus (CRVS) shares are attractively positioned, as the stock, at its current valuation, has yet to reflect soquelitinib’s potential against atopic dermatitis and other related autoimmune conditions, as well as peripheral T-cell lymphoma.

The analyst noted that all those programs have catalysts scheduled for the next 12-18 months.

Choi identified soquelitinib as one of the most promising candidates for refractory atopic dermatitis, a condition with a large unmet need for next-gen therapies, according to key opinion leaders.

"While it is still at an early developmental stage, we can envision CRVS leveraging soquelitinib and gradually maturing into large-cap biotech over the long term," the analyst added.

data.title: Cohen & Steers expects 40% compensation ratio and mid-single-digit G&A growth for 2026 amid ETF expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cohen & Steers (CNS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Yesterday, we reported earnings of $0.79 per share as compared to $0.81 sequentially. Revenue for Q1 increased from the prior quarter by 0.3% to $144.3 million." (Interim CFO Michael Donohue)

  • "Ending AUM in Q1 was $93.1 billion, which was up from $90.5 billion at the end of Q4." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "The first quarter's fundamental highlights include net inflows of $497 million a strong one unfunded pipeline of $1.7 billion, characterized by good velocity with continued fundings and new mandates, stable fee rates strong absolute performance and neutral relative performance." (CEO Joseph Harvey)

  • "Our corporate strategy for active ETFs is going very well. Total AUM for our first five ETFs is currently $675 million." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Our nontraded REIT Cohen & Steers Income Opportunities REIT has established a portfolio of 11 properties owned or under contract totaling $650 million in assets and continues to provide investment performance at the top of the real estate peer group with 10.6% annualized returns since inception against the 4.3% peer average." (CEO Harvey)

  • "On a 1-year basis, 86% of our AUM has outperformed its benchmark, while our 3- and 5-year outperformance rates are both above 97%. 95% of our open-end fund AUM is rated 4- or 5-star by Morningstar, which is up from 90% last quarter." (President & Chief Investment Officer John Cheigh)

Outlook

  • "With respect to compensation and benefits, we would expect our compensation ratio to remain at 40% as we experienced in Q1." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "We expect G&A to increase in the mid-single digits for the year as compared to the prior year." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "Lastly, regarding 2026 guidance, we expect our effective tax rate to remain consistent at 25.5% on an as-adjusted basis." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "We announced the conversion of our future of energy open-end fund to an ETF which should occur sometime midyear." (CEO Harvey)

Financial Results

  • "Operating income was $50.7 million during the quarter compared to $52.4 million sequentially. The and our operating margin was 35.1% compared to 36.4% in the prior quarter." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "Our liquidity totaled $343 million at quarter end, which represents a decrease of $60 million versus the prior period." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "Our active ETFs continued their momentum with $224 million of third-party net flows in the quarter." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Sub-advisory experienced $269 million of net outflows in the quarter with $164 million in outflows from Japan." (CEO Harvey)

Q&A

  • John Dunn, Evercore ISI: "You mentioned it's been 2 straight inflow quarters" in advisory and asked whether it was sustainable and driven by new or existing clients; CEO Harvey: "we now have a very strong pipeline, I think, for the third straight quarter at $1.7 billion" and "we were awarded $574 million of new mandates" while describing "good velocity" and saying, "we're optimistic that the institutional advisory channel will continue to perform better and better."

  • John Dunn, Evercore ISI: Asked on active ETFs, including "any cannibalization" and demand pockets; CEO Harvey: "The tone in active ETFs is very good" and "in our business projections for the ETFs, we've factored in some the cannibalization, so to speak" while adding, "I would say I'm very bullish on this vehicle" and noting, "we received our first placement on a major broker-dealer platform."

  • John Dunn, Evercore ISI: Asked whether global real estate interest was rising; CEO Harvey: "We've seen more interest and flows into our global real estate strategy. So I would expect that to continue."

  • Macrae Sykes, Gabelli Funds: Asked what catalysts drive bigger platform shifts into real estate; CIO Cheigh: "it's a combination of investors thinking about the interest rate cycle as well as the fundamental or supply and demand cycle" and "the fundamental inflection is probably the bigger thing that our investors are focused on."

  • Macrae Sykes, Gabelli Funds: Asked how CNS competes with private-credit products given adviser incentives; CEO Harvey: "we think about every morning we would get up is delivering investment performance and managing risk," and an Unknown Executive added, "we've made it very investor-friendly compared with the peer group."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative: John Dunn pressed on sustainability and product mechanics, including "more sustainable" advisory flows and "any cannibalization," while Macrae Sykes focused on competitive distribution friction, asking, "how do you sort of compete" with adviser incentives.

  • Management slightly positive: CEO Harvey used confident language on key initiatives, including "I would say I'm very bullish on this vehicle" (ETFs) and "we're optimistic that the institutional advisory channel will continue to perform better and better," while also framing macro uncertainty: "consensus postwar is force stagflation with the key unknowables being how much and for how long."

  • Versus last quarter: In Q4 2025, management highlighted macro rotation with broad confidence, including "we expect economic activity, and market returns to broaden" (CIO Cheigh), while Q1 2026 incorporated a sharper geopolitical overlay, including "the world changed with U.S. military operations in Iran" (CEO Harvey).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "Revenue for Q1 increased from the prior quarter by 0.3% to $144.3 million" (Interim CFO Donohue) compared with Q4 2025: "Revenue for Q4 increased 2% sequentially to $143.8 million." (Interim CFO Donohue)

  • "Our corporate strategy for active ETFs is going very well. Total AUM for our first five ETFs is currently $675 million" (CEO Harvey) compared with Q4 2025: "We closed the year with 5 ETFs and total AUM of $378 million." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Sub-advisory experienced $269 million of net outflows in the quarter" (CEO Harvey) compared with Q4 2025: "Subadvisory had $30 million of net inflows." (CEO Harvey)

  • "We would expect our compensation ratio to remain at 40%" and "G&A to increase in the mid-single digits" (Interim CFO Donohue) compared with Q4 2025: "we would expect our compensation ratio to remain at 40%" and "projecting [G&A] to be in the mid-single-digit percentage range." (Interim CFO Donohue)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Business activity slowed for a period as investors attempted to calibrate how long the conflict will last and what the short- and long-term ramifications could be for economies, geopolitics and asset allocation." (CEO Harvey)

  • "A key question for CNS REIT short term is how redemption constraints in private wealth vehicles will affect investor appetite for evergreen vehicles generally." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Sub-advisory experienced $269 million of net outflows in the quarter with $164 million in outflows from Japan." (CEO Harvey)

  • "Preferred securities and fixed income classes broadly declined slightly in the quarter as renewed inflation concerns indicate that monetary policy could be tighter for longer." (President & CIO Cheigh)

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized modest sequential declines in profitability alongside higher AUM, positioning Q1 around continued positive net flows and a $1.7 billion pipeline while expanding distribution and ETFs. Leadership repeatedly framed investor behavior through the lens of geopolitical shock and liquidity sensitivity, with CEO Joseph Harvey tying product demand to a preference for “hard assets” and liquidity, highlighting ETF scaling, the planned midyear fund-to-ETF conversion, and continued focus on 2026 expense and tax guidance (40% compensation ratio, mid-single-digit G&A growth, and a 25.5% effective tax rate on an as-adjusted basis).

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Brent oil prices will settle around $80 in the next years – analyst
data.description:
Brent oil prices (CO1:COM) are expected to settle around $80 per barrel over the next two years, according to Warren Pies, co-founder of 3Fourteen Research.

The forecast comes as markets react to the announcement that the Strait of Hormuz is reopening following a period of heightened uncertainty.

In an interview with CNBC, Pies explained that the reopening allows the market to avoid what could have been a far more severe supply disruption.

He noted that the longer the strait remained closed, the higher the risk of elevated long-term oil prices, making the resolution a significant relief for energy markets.

While current prices remain slightly above the $80 level, Pies said the market tends to reach equilibrium quickly.

“I think long term, Brent’s going to settle in around $80 a barrel,” he said, adding that this average would likely hold for the next two years.

Beyond oil, Pies argued that the declining crude prices (CO1:COM), (CL1:COM) strengthen the case for equities (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI).

He described the development as something that “amplifies the equity bull case,” pointing to recent positive momentum in the broader stock market as evidence that a new rally may be underway.

Supporting his bullish outlook, Pies highlighted that earnings have grown 9% year-to-date even as the market experienced a correction. He noted that the price-to-earnings ratio corrected by 18%, a rare occurrence outside of Fed tightening or a recession, which he views as a reset that positions the market for further gains.

Despite the recent volatility, 3Fourteen Research is maintaining its year-end index target of 7,850 for the S&P 500 (SP500). Pies concluded that current conditions, including reset positioning and improving fundamentals, have “the looks of the next leg of the bull market.”

data.title: U.S. stocks rise to new highs as the Strait of Hormuz is declared open
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages jumped on Friday to record highs after Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz open for commercial vessels. President Donald Trump, however, said the naval blockade will remain in effect.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +1.4%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.6%, and the Dow (DJI) was +2.2%.

The Ports and Maritime Organization of the Islamic Republic of Iran said the Strait of Hormuz is now “completely open” for all commercial vessels for the rest of the Lebanon ceasefire timeline. This comes after Israel and Lebanon announced a 10-day ceasefire on Thursday.

Nonetheless, Trump said that the naval blockade on Iranian ports will remain in full force and effect. The reopening of the passageway was one of the conditions of the fragile two-week ceasefire that is currently in effect.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) rapidly decreased to $83, while Brent (CO1:COM) also dropped to $89 per barrel.

In addition, the U.S. dollar (DXY) retreated sharply, -0.3%, erasing its recent Middle East conflict-driven gains as the Strait of Hormuz was declared open to commercial ships by Iran.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 9 basis points lower at 4.23%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 10 basis points to 3.69%.

“The reported reopening of the Strait of Hormuz by Iran is undoubtedly a welcome relief for the global economy. Needless to say, its true impact depends on the durability of this reopening, which will require sustained confidence-building measures from all three warring parties,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

“Equally critical is the reaction of ship operators. While their immediate priority is to bring out vessels currently in the region as soon as they can, the real litmus test lies in their willingness to send new ships in. Their return is necessary for restoring the global energy flow and bringing regional production sites fully back online,” El-Erian added.

data.title: Ally expects 2026 NIM of 3.60%-3.70% while reiterating retail auto NCOs of 1.8%-2.0%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ally Financial (ALLY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "One quarter into 2026, our results confirm we're on the right path and support my confidence in our outlook even as the macro environment remains dynamic." (CEO Michael Rhodes)
  • "Adjusted EPS of $1.11 was up 90% year-over-year." (CEO Rhodes)
  • "Margin of 3.52% was impacted by the lease headwinds we discussed last quarter, but we remain confident in our ability to deliver a sustainable upper 3% margin, the final lever of our mid-teens thesis." (CEO Rhodes)
  • "4.4 million applications reflect another record quarter" and "consumer originations of $11.5 billion, up 13% year-over-year despite a decline in industry light vehicle sales and healthy competition." (CEO Rhodes)
  • "Written premium of $389 million, marking a first quarter record for Ally." (CEO Rhodes)
  • "In Corporate Finance, we delivered a 26% ROE while growing the portfolio to $13.7 billion, up roughly 6% quarter-over-quarter." (CEO Rhodes)
  • "We ended the quarter with $146 billion in retail deposit balances" and "Retail deposits continue to represent nearly 90% of total funding and 92% are FDIC insured." (CEO Rhodes)
  • "Net financing revenue, excluding OID of $1.6 billion was up 8% year-over-year" and "Adjusted provision expense of $474 million was down $23 million year-over-year." (CFO Russell Hutchinson)
  • "Earlier this week, we announced a quarterly dividend of $0.30 for the second quarter of 2026" and "we repurchased shares worth $147 million." (CFO Hutchinson)

Outlook

  • "Guidance remains consistent with what we shared 3 months ago." (CFO Hutchinson)
  • "For the year, we expect 3.60% to 3.70%" and "as you do the mathematics on that, that very much implies that we exit the year at or above the high end of that range." (CFO Hutchinson)
  • "We've left our retail auto NCO guide for '26 unchanged at 1.8% to 2%." (CFO Hutchinson)
  • "Movement in benchmark rates may impact the timing and pace of future NIM expansion" and "our baseline assumptions reflect the March 31 forward curve, which doesn't include a Fed funds cut until June of 2027." (CFO Hutchinson)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted net revenue of $2.2 billion was up 6% year-over-year and 12% when adjusting for the sale of credit card." (CEO Michael Rhodes)
  • "Net interest margin, excluding OID, was 3.52%" and "Lease yield included a $10 million loss on lease terminations given the headwinds on select plug-in hybrids we noted in January." (CFO Russell Hutchinson)
  • "Retail deposit balances increased $2.6 billion, and we added 74,000 net new customers" and "we were able to reduce liquid saving rates by 10 basis points in February, bringing our cumulative beta to 57%." (CFO Hutchinson)
  • "While not reflected in 1Q results, we just reduced liquid savings another 10 basis points, bringing our cumulative beta to 63%." (CFO Hutchinson)
  • "Consolidated net charge-offs of 121 basis points were down 13 basis points versus the prior quarter" and "Retail auto net charge-offs of 197 basis points were down 17 basis points quarter-over-quarter." (CFO Hutchinson)
  • "Retail auto coverage rate was flat at 3.75%." (CFO Hutchinson)

Q&A

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: asked about consumer pressure and credit expectations; CEO Michael Rhodes: "on the whole, it has yet to materially impact our business" and "we're prioritizing, I call it, discipline over volume."
  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: asked about NIM cadence and deposit-cost management; CFO Russell Hutchinson: "our guidance is now based on an assumption that Fed funds will be flat through the rest of this year" and "we've maintained our guide at 3.60% to 3.70%."
  • Robert Wildhack, Autonomous Research: asked whether buybacks could be maintained/accelerated under revised Basel; CFO Russell Hutchinson: "they are proposals" and "our capital priorities remain unchanged."
  • Robert Wildhack, Autonomous Research: asked about competition in retail auto and deposits; CFO Hutchinson: "there really haven't been recent changes in the competitive environment"; CEO Rhodes: "we're very pleased with what we're seeing" and "we're a digital bank with a national brand."
  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: asked about reserve coverage progression and whether Ally may lean into growth; CFO Hutchinson: "We held reserves flat at 3.75%" and "we don't predicate that on the idea of reserve releases"; CEO Rhodes: "as data ebbs and flows, we'll make some adjustments."
  • Brian Foran, Truist: asked whether opting into ERBA could be beneficial; CFO Hutchinson: "we're very focused on evaluating both ERBA and RSA" and "we will be kind of automatically put into the RSA bucket."
  • Brian Foran, Truist: pressed on what drives Corporate Finance growth and underwriting posture; CFO Hutchinson: "we don't chase growth" and "we're not making compromises based on credit here for the sake of growth"; CEO Rhodes: "a lot of the growth you're seeing is just us growing with our clients."
  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: asked whether retail auto performance improves through 2026; CFO Hutchinson: "1.8% to 2% is a very kind of balanced view of what we expect for 2026."
  • John Pancari, Evercore: asked about lease residual losses and earning asset growth; CFO Hutchinson: "our outlook for the year very much takes into account" remaining PHEV pressure and "we continue to feel really good about our guidance around earning assets."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, centered on macro/consumer resilience, credit durability, deposit competition, capital rules, and private credit scrutiny, including questions framed around "a lot out there that the consumer is facing" (Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs) and "a lot of investor nervousness in the space" (Brian Foran, Truist).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, pairing confidence with caution, including "we remain confident" (CEO Michael Rhodes) alongside "we're choosing to be deliberately measured" (CEO Rhodes).
  • Versus last quarter: management kept a similar confidence-through-discipline framing, but updated the rate backdrop more explicitly, with "our baseline assumptions... doesn't include a Fed funds cut until June of 2027" (CFO Russell Hutchinson) compared with the prior call’s assumptions that included Fed cuts in 2026.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Management repeated the Focused. Forward strategy framing, but Q1 put more emphasis on record application flow and deposit betas, with "4.4 million applications" (CEO Michael Rhodes) following Q4’s focus on full-year execution and exiting noncore businesses.
  • NIM commentary shifted from Q4’s expectation that "we expect NIM to be slightly down on a sequential basis" (CFO Russell Hutchinson, Q4) to Q1’s positioning that "we've maintained our guide at 3.60% to 3.70%" under an assumption of flat Fed funds for the rest of 2026 (CFO Hutchinson, Q1).
  • Capital-return messaging evolved from Q4’s explicit "low and slow" buyback language to Q1’s emphasis on flexibility, with "our open-ended buyback authorization continues to provide flexibility" (CFO Hutchinson).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Lease yield included a $10 million loss on lease terminations" tied to "headwinds on select plug-in hybrids" and Ally said it "accelerated depreciation on certain leases maturing in the near term." (CFO Russell Hutchinson)
  • "We remain disciplined on pricing through a key growth period" and "we anticipate a decline in 2Q retail deposit balances given seasonal tax payments." (CFO Hutchinson)
  • "Given headlines related to the private credit industry more broadly" Ally highlighted: "We've never recorded a loss since we entered the business in 2019, and no loan has ever been classified as criticized or placed on nonaccrual." (CFO Hutchinson)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as confirming momentum under its Focused. Forward strategy, highlighting $1.11 adjusted EPS, $2.2B adjusted net revenue, and a 3.52% NIM shaped by lease headwinds. Guidance was reiterated, including 2026 NIM of 3.60%-3.70% and retail auto NCOs of 1.8%-2.0%, while executives emphasized measured underwriting, ongoing deposit repricing actions, and continued capital return through a $0.30 dividend and $147M of buybacks, with constructive—but still-proposed—Basel III revisions viewed as supportive of Ally’s capital priorities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Iran agrees to suspend nuclear program, Trump says - report
data.description:
President Donald Trump said Friday that Iran has agreed to suspend its nuclear program indefinitely and will not receive any frozen funds from the U.S. as part of a potential agreement to end the war in the Middle East.

In a phone interview with Bloomberg, Trump said a deal to end the conflict is mostly complete. The president said talks over a lasting deal will “probably” be held over the weekend. “Most of the main points are finalized. It’ll go pretty quickly,” the president said.

Iran has not yet commented on any deal beyond the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, nor on claims made by Trump on Thursday that Tehran had offered concessions, including over the key issue of its nuclear program.

Trump said he has not decided who would lead a U.S. delegation for talks with Iranian officials to sign a deal, Bloomberg reported. When asked if he would travel to Pakistan, which hosted the first round of negotiations, the president said, “I may.”

Vice President JD Vance led discussions with Iranian officials last weekend. Trump said he was considering sending Vance alongside his son-in-law, Jared Kushner, and envoy Steve Witkoff for the upcoming talks.

The president denied that the moratorium on Iran’s nuclear program would expire after 20 years. When asked if the program will completely halt, Trump responded, “No years, unlimited.”

data.title: Fed's policy will depend on length of Middle East conflict, San Francisco Fed's Daly
data.description:
Uncertainty continues to loom over a solid U.S. economy, San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly said Friday. That makes it difficult to provide a sense of what the Federal Reserve will do next in interest rates, she added.

"The fundamentals of the economy are quite solid," she said at the University of California, Berkeley's Fisher Center for Real Estate & Urban Economics' Spring 2026 Policy Advisory Board meeting.

The Fed's rate path will depend largely on how long the Middle East conflict lasts. If it ends quickly and oil prices go down, then "we're back on that path for interest rates where we were before," and there's no lasting damage to that path.

What she worries about the most is that persistently higher oil prices will “chip away” at consumers' ability to buy other things.

Daly describes the current federal funds rate target range of 3.50%-3.75% as "slightly restrictive."

Monetary policy is currently in a "very good place," she said. "Right now, it's not constraining the economy so much that the labor economy is faltering, not letting go of the reins completely so that inflation has no bridle."

The central bank can leave policy where it is, which would still restrain inflation, for the year. If inflation took off, which is not her modal outlook, then the Fed could raise. If  the labor market falters or the conflict ends soon, then it could adjust down.

Another factor that may eventually factor into rate decisions is productivity growth and artificial intelligence.

Productivity is rising. "That's a good thing for the economy." Daly said.

Domestic labor growth is near zero. Combined with almost no immigration growth, that means the U.S.'s labor force growth is near zero. Many businesses that rely on immigrants for their labor force are adopting more labor-saving technology, she said.

Regarding AI, "no technology has ever reduced employment, on net, ever," Daly said. "It changes who gets employed and what skills get employed."

Productivity may help with disinflationary pressure.

data.title: DeepSeek enters talks to raise $300M to better compete with frontier AI labs: report
data.description:
DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) has initiated its first fundraising campaign in hopes of raising $300M for a valuation of more than $10B, according to The Information.

The China-based company intends to enhance its funding to better take on AI rivals such as OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO), the report said. The additional funds could help DeepSeek hire more AI experts and build more capable models.

DeepSeek rocked the tech sector last year when it claimed to develop a model nearly as powerful as ChatGPT or Claude but with only a fraction of the computing costs. 

However, its market share has rapidly declined over the past 12 months. In April 2025, DeepSeek held 10% of the daily average user market share among chatbots, according to a tracker by Apptopia. However, that had declined to 3.3% by March 2026.

data.title: IBM appears set up to top first quarter estimates as it builds on AI narrative: analysts
data.description:
IBM (IBM) appears poised to surpass consensus estimates when it releases its first quarter 2026 financial results post-market on Wednesday, April 22, as it builds on its AI strategy, according to analysts.

"While the prior year ended with a broad-based beat in Q4, the software outperformance came despite slower than expected growth from Hybrid Cloud, aka Red Hat," said RBC Capital Markets analysts, led by Matthew Swanson, in an investor note. "We'd expect things to pick up where they left off with a top-line beat driven by infrastructure and software strength being driven by Automation and Transaction Processing."

Although Red Hat growth slowed to 8% from 12% in constant currency during the fourth quarter of 2025, IBM expects double-digit growth in 2026.

RBC maintained its Outperform rating but reduced its price target to $330 from $361.

Wedbush also retained its Outperform rating and $330 price target ahead of earnings.

"Recent announcements continue to reinforce IBM's AI narrative heading into the print," said Wedbush analysts, led by Dan Ives, in a Friday investor note. "On April 1, IBM stated that 11 AI and automation software solutions received FedRAMP authorization, with the solutions deployed on AWS GovCloud to support secure and compliant government use cases, expanding IBM's footprint in federal and regulated markets. IBM also recently announced new cybersecurity measures to help enterprises confront agentic attacks, including a cybersecurity assessment and IBM Autonomous Security, a multi-agent-powered service designed to deliver coordinated response and intelligence at machine speed, further broadening IBM's AI value proposition into security."

During the call, RBC analysts expect IBM to address concerns related to Anthropic's (ANTHRO) COBOL modernization solution. They also expect to hear more about the Confluent acquisition as well as quantum computing initiatives.

A consensus estimate expects IBM to report adjusted EPS of $1.81 and GAAP EPS of $1.37. The revenue estimate is $15.64B. During the same quarter in 2025, IBM reported adjusted EPS of $1.60 on revenue of $14.54B.
